coredata.dc:title: Spectrofluorimetric determination of vitamin D in the serum of autistic and healthy children using functionalized graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Almalki, Atiah H.
Alaqel, Saleh I.
Alharbi, Adnan
Abduljabbar, Maram H.
Bamaga, Ahmed K.
Alsenani, Faisal
Ramzy, Sherif
coredata.dc:description: Vitamin D is one of the most essential nutrients for brain development, and deficiencies during pregnancy and early childhood development might be associated with autism. Regular monitoring of serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 level could help in early diagnosis and therapy. Analytical measurement of serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 level using the traditional matrix-matched calibration technique yields inaccurate results due to absence of serum matrix free from 25-hydroxyvitamin D3. The aim of this work was to develop a validated spectrofluorimetric methodology based on the standard addition approach for quantifying 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 levels in real serum samples of autistic children. The spectrofluorimetric methodology utilizes functionalized graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for selective quantification of 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 level, which is based on measuring the quenching properties of 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 on a fluorescent probe. The standard addition approach exhibits a minimal matrix interference since it identically utilizes the same matrix of each study sample for creating its own calibration curve. The method was validated using the guidelines outlined in ICH M10 draft for endogenous compounds quantification. The method was successfully applied for quantifying the serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 levels in autistic and healthy children, and autistic children had significantly lower serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 levels (with a mean ± SD of 23.80 ± 17.19) when compared to healthy children (with a mean ± SD of 50.13 ± 18.74, P < 0.001). These results suggested an association between vitamin D deficiency and autism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Autism
vitamin D
Spectrofluorimetric methodology
The standard addition approach
Functionalized graphene quantum dots

Functionalized GQD fluorescence characteristics and 25(OH)D3 quenching principles
Rational for application of standard addition approach
Standard addition approach calibration curves and application for estimating the endogenous 25(OH)D3 levels is autistic children and healthy children

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Example of standard addition approach calibration curve for 25(OH)D3 in real serum sample (dilution factor, 1:10 v/v).
Excitation (−−−−) and emission (ــــــ) spectra of the functionalized GQDs.
Emission spectra of the functionalized GQDs alone and after adding ascending concentrations of 25(OH)D3.
Comparison between autistic and healthy groups regarding serum 25(OH)D3 concentration.
Comparison between autistic and healthy groups regarding serum 25(OH)D3 concentration.

head.ce:title: Spectrofluorimetric determination of vitamin D in the serum of autistic and healthy children using functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Vitamin D is one of the most essential nutrients for brain development, and deficiencies during pregnancy and early childhood development might be associated with autism. Regular monitoring of serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 level could help in early diagnosis and therapy. Analytical measurement of serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 level using the traditional matrix-matched calibration technique yields inaccurate results due to absence of serum matrix free from 25-hydroxyvitamin D3. The aim of this work was to develop a validated spectrofluorimetric methodology based on the standard addition approach for quantifying 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 levels in real serum samples of autistic children. The spectrofluorimetric methodology utilizes functionalized graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for selective quantification of 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 level, which is based on measuring the quenching properties of 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 on a fluorescent probe. The standard addition approach exhibits a minimal matrix interference since it identically utilizes the same matrix of each study sample for creating its own calibration curve. The method was validated using the guidelines outlined in ICH M10 draft for endogenous compounds quantification. The method was successfully applied for quantifying the serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 levels in autistic and healthy children, and autistic children had significantly lower serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 levels (with a mean ± SD of 23.80 ± 17.19) when compared to healthy children (with a mean ± SD of 50.13 ± 18.74, P < 0.001). These results suggested an association between vitamin D deficiency and autism.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Vitamin D is essential for infant brain development, and deficiencies during pregnancy, neonatal, and early childhood may be linked to a variety of mental and behavioral disorders, including autism [1,2]. Several clinical studies suggested an association between vitamin D deficiency and autism [3–7]. Accordingly, the development of a validated analytical technique capable of determining vitamin D concentrations in the real blood samples of autistic individuals may be beneficial in facilitating early monitoring of deficiency and quick treatment.

Vitamin D3 is the main form of circulating vitamin D. It is produced in the skin by solar UV irradiation of 7-dehydrocholesterol, which is subsequently metabolized in the liver and kidney to its principal circulatory form, 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 (25(OH)D3), and endocrine form, 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3 (1,25(OH)2D3), respectively [8].

Serum 25(OH)D3 has been recognised as a universal biomarker for assessing vitamin D levels [9]. There is no unanimity on what defines vitamin D deficiency; nonetheless, various organizations define it differently based on serum levels of 25(OH)D3. Vitamin D deficiency, insufficiency, and optimal are defined by the Endocrine Society as serum 25(OH)D3 levels of ≤ 20 ng/ml, 21–29 ng/ml, and ≥ 30 ng/ml, respectively [10].

Several chromatographic techniques with different detection systems [9,11–17] have been published to assess the amount of circulating 25(OH)D3. Although these techniques are sensitive and reliable, they have various limitations, including the high cost and complexity of the analytical apparatus and operation, as well as the significant matrix effect.

The natural presence of 25(OH)D3 as an endogenous compound of blood, as well as the absence of serum matrix free from endogenous 25(OH)D3, presents a considerable obstacle to the development of a validated analytical method for quantifying this analyte based on the traditional matrix-matched calibration approach. To address this obstacle, the International Council for Harmonization (ICH) specified four alternative approaches for calculating endogenous analyte concentrations in its draft ICH guideline M10 on bioanalytical technique validation, which was released in 2019 [18]. These approaches were the surrogate matrix approach, the surrogate analyte approach, the background subtraction approach, and the standard addition approach.

This study aimed to develop a validated spectrofluorimetric method for monitoring and quantifying 25(OH)D3 levels in the serum of autistic and healthy children with minimal matrix effect by incorporating the standard addition approach. A novel feature of this work is functionalization of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by 25(OH)D3-tetraphenylborate ion association complex to optimize its selectivity for 25(OH)D3 quantification. The spectrofluorimetric procedure is based on measuring the quenching responses caused by the dynamic interaction of 25(OH)D3 with the functionalized GQDs. This method shows excellent applicability to quantify 25(OH)D3 in real serum samples and has been used to compare the plasma levels of 25(OH)D3 in autistics and healthy children. The findings show that autistic children have considerably lower plasma 25(OH)D3 levels than healthy children, supporting the belief that there is a link between vitamin D insufficiency and autism.

A well-characterized, authentic GQDs powder was provided by Sigma-Aldrich (USA). The standard 25(OH)D3 and sodium tetraphenylborate powders were supplied from Sigma-Aldrich, (Germany). All solvents and chemicals utilized were of the highest analytical purity. Following USP guidelines, buffer solutions with varying pH values were developed.
Measurements were conducted using a Jasco FP-6200 spectrofluorometer (Japan) fitted with a DC powered 150-Watt Xenon lamp.

The stock solution of standard 25(OH)D3 was prepared in ethanol at a concentration of 100 µg/mL. The working solution was made at a final concentration of 1 µg/mL by further dilution of the stock solution with distilled water. Three working solutions labeled as “standard addition spiking solutions” were prepared at concentrations of 50 ng/mL, 200 ng/mL, and 600 ng/mL. The solution of sodium tetraphenylborate, 10-2 M, was prepared in water.

The ion association precipitate of 25(OH)D3-tetraphenylborate was produced by adding 50 mL of 10−2 M sodium tetraphenylborate solutions to a conical flask containing 50 mL of 10−2 M 25(OH)D3 solution acidified with 2 drops of 1 M HCl. After soaking with their mother liquor for 8 h, the precipitate was filtered and rinsed thoroughly with distilled water before being allowed to dry at room temperature for 24 h.

A weighed GQDs powder equal to 100 mg was mixed with 300 mg of 25(OH)D3-tetraphenylborate complex powder and transferred into a conical flask. The powder was then dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water before being sonicated for 2 h. The solution was filtered and then subjected to dialysis for 6 h with distilled water before being transferred into a 100-mL volumetric flask. The flask was adjusted to the volume with distilled water and stored at 4 °C. The fluorescence intensity of the functionalized GQDs (F0 ) was measured at 440 nm after excitation at 350 nm.

The blood samples were collected from the children participating in this study at Al-Azhar University Hospital in New Damietta, Egypt, after their parents supplied written informed consent. Two age-matched groups (from 4 to 12 years) of children were recruited. The first group consists of 18 proven autistic children, whereas the second group consists of 10 healthy children. The blood samples were allowed to coagulate before being centrifuged, and the supernatants (serum) were collected and stored at −20 °C until analysis.
The method was validated using the guidelines outlined in ICH M10 draft for endogenous compounds quantification [18], as well as the applications by Hasegawa et al. [19] and Coglianese et al. [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard addition approach calibration curves; calculating the study samples (endogenous 25(OH)D3) concentrations
ce:section.ce:para:
The calibration curve for each study sample was constructed by plotting the fluorescence difference (ΔF) against the sample concentrations [non-spiked sample (0 ng/mL), and three spiked sample (5 ng/mL, 20 ng/mL, 60 ng/mL)]. The standard addition approach equation is defined as y = ax + b, where (a) represents the slope of the standard addition approach calibration curve and (b) represents the value of the standard addition approach curve's intercept on the y axis. The intercept value (b) reflects the non-spiked sample's fluorescence difference (ΔF), which is larger than zero due to the existence of an unknown endogenous quantity of the 25(OH)D3 in the original matrix. When y = 0, the endogenous 25(OH)D3 concentration in non-spiked sample (x) is estimated as the negative intercept on the calibration curve's x-axis using the following equation:
(1)
x
=
-
b / a
Additionally, for each spiked sample level (n) of the calibration curve, the endogenous 25(OH)D3 concentration (xn ) was also calculated. First the intercept values for each spiked sample level (bn ) were calculated using the following formula:
(2)
b
n
=
a
∗
x
+
n
-
y
n
Where (x+n ) represents the nominal concentration spiked to each spiked sample (5 ng/mL, 20 ng/mL, 60 ng/mL) and (yn ) represents the fluorescence difference (ΔF) response for each spiked sample in the calibration curve.

An example of the standard addition approach calibration curve and the mathematical calculation of the mean endogenous 25(OH)D3 concentration can be seen in Fig. 1 . The mean endogenous 25(OH)D3 concentration in this sample would be recorded as 4.98 ng/mL, but after accounting for the 1:10 (v/v) dilution factor, the final reported value would be 49.80 ng/mL.

Accuracy and precision were evaluated by quantifying the 25(OH)D3 concentration spiked to each spiked sample of the calibration curve (c+n ). The standard addition approach calibration curve was analyzed in triplicate (n = 3), and the endogenous 25(OH)D3 concentration of each spiked sample (xn ) was calculated as mentioned above. Then the 25(OH)D3 concentration spiked to each spiked sample of the calibration curve (c+n ) was calculated from the following equation:
(3)
c
+
n
=
y
n
/
a
-
x
n
The percent recovery at each spiked sample level was calculated from the following equation:
(4)
%
R
+
n
=
c
+
n
/
x
+
n
∗ 100
The accuracy and precision at each spiked sample level were calculated as the mean percent recovery (%R) and relative standard deviation (RSD), respectively, of three determinations (n = 3) on the same day for the intra-day accuracy and precision and on three days for the inter-day accuracy and precision. Accepted accuracy result should be within ± 15 % of the nominal 25(OH)D3 concentration, whereas accepted precision (RSD) should be less than 15 %.

In the absence of a blank matrix, determining the lower limit of quantification (LLOQ) is difficult. As a result, we're examining the instrumental LLOQ in a neat blank matrix (water) and the method LLOQ in a real serum matrix. A series of analyte samples were prepared at concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 10 ng/mL in water and tested in triplicate for instrumental LLOQ. The instrumental LLOQ was recognized as the lowest concentration that yields a fluorescence difference (ΔF) more than 5. On the other hand, the method LLOQ was recognized as the lowest analyte concentration spiked to the serum sample that can be detected with acceptable accuracy and precision results. The serum sample used for the method LLOQ evaluation was derived from a pool of different serum samples with low concentrations of 25(OH)D3. The pool serum sample was broken down into five equal-volume (1 mL) samples. Four spiked samples were prepared with final 25(OH)D3 added concentrations: 1, 2, 3 and 4 ng/mL, while the fourth sample was left non-spiked. The procedure for standard addition approach was completed and the accuracy and precision of three determinations (n = 3) were calculated for each spiked sample level.

The aim of this work is to develop a validated and highly sensitive spectrofluorimetric method that incorporated the standard addition approach for quantifying 25(OH)D3 levels in real blood samples. The method utilizes a functionalized GQDs as a fluorescent probe for selective quantification of 25(OH)D3 levels, which is based on measuring the quenching properties of 25(OH)D3 on a fluorescent probe. The method was successfully applied for quantifying the serum 25(OH)D3 levels in autistic and healthy participants, and autistic participants had significantly lower serum 25(OH)D3 levels when compared to healthy persons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalized GQD fluorescence characteristics and 25(OH)D3 quenching principles
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, an authentic GQDs was functionalized with a 25(OH)D3-tetraphenylborate ion association complex to optimize its selectivity for 25(OH)D3 quantification. The functionalized GQDs has distinctive fluorescence characteristics with emission and excitation wavelengths of 440 and 350 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 ). The fluorescence spectrum of the functionalized GQDs was selectively quenched upon interacting with 25(OH)D3 in concentration-dependent responses. The fluorescence intensity of the functionalized GQDs gradually decreased as the concentration of 25(OH)D3 increased, as seen in Fig. 3 . The quenching mechanism that explains how 25(OH)D3 interacts with functionalized GQDs and suppresses their fluorescence spectra must be elucidated. Static quenching, dynamic quenching, and the inner filter effect are examples of probable mechanisms. The UV–vis spectrum of 25(OH)D3 shows no absorption peaks in the wavelength range from 600 to 300 nm, ruling out the inner filter effect of the 25(OH)D3 absorption properties. A temperature dependence experiment was carried out to determine whether the quenching mechanism is dynamic or static using the Stern-Volmer equation. The Stern-Volmer equation was defined as F 0/F=1+Ksv [Q], where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the functionalized GQDs before and after addition of 25(OH)D3, KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant (slope), and Q is the 25(OH)D3 concentration. In the case of dynamic quenching, higher temperatures increase the diffusion coefficient and energy transfer, resulting in a rise in the Stern-Volmer quenching constant value. On the other hand, for static quenching, increasing temperatures would result in a decrease in the rate constant of quenching due to lower stability of the non-fluorescent complex, resulting in a decrease in the Stern-Volmer quenching constant value. The Stern-Volmer graphs in Fig. 4 demonstrate that when temperature increased the Stern-Volmer quenching constant value increased, suggesting that the quenching mechanism is dynamic.

The effect of buffer pH, volume of functionalized GQDs, diluting solvent, and reaction time on the fluorescence quenching reaction were studied and optimized. The buffer pH effect was investigated across a wide pH range, and it was discovered that a buffer solution with a pH of 8 achieved consistent quenching. Furthermore, multiple volumes of pH 8 buffer solution were examined, with 1 mL yielding the best results. The effect of functionalized GQDs concentration was examined using various volumes of the functionalized GQDs solution, with 1 mL producing the optimal fluorescence quenching efficiency throughout a wide dynamic range. Because altering the diluting solvents had no effect on quenching efficiency, water was employed in this procedure. Additionally, the quenching rate increased with time and became almost constant after 10 min, showing that all 25(OH)D3 had occupied the functionalized GQDs binding sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rational for application of standard addition approach
ce:section.ce:para: Analytical measurement of analytes that are also endogenous components of the biological matrix, such as vitamin D, is difficult, especially when there is a substantial variability in endogenous analyte levels in the studied population and absence of serum matrix free from endogenous 25(OH)D3. The ICH-M10 guidelines [18] outline many techniques to overcome this difficulty, such as surrogate matrix, surrogate analyte, background subtraction, and standard addition approach.

In the surrogate matrix approach the calibration standards and quality control samples (QCs) are investigated in synthetic matrix that mimics the biological matrix of the studied samples [21,22]. The main constraint of this approach is the struggle of synthesizing a matrix that is closely matched to the biological one and therefore may impact the matrix effect, resulting in conflicting results [20]. The calibration standards and quality control samples (QCs) in the other three approaches, on the other hand, are probed in the same biological matrix as the studied samples, potentially decreasing the matrix impact. The surrogate analyte approach's use may be limited due to the high cost of the isotope labelled analytes utilized to generate the calibration curve [20,23]. Furthermore, the background subtraction strategy may be infeasible when the analyte concentration varies greatly in the population and when the endogenous analytes background signal is significant [20,24].

The standard addition approach treats each study sample as an independent entity with its own calibration curve, where each study sample is broken down into equal-volume samples. Except for one, all samples are independently spiked with known quantities of analyte standards to create a calibration curve for each study sample. The concentration of each study sample is then calculated as the negative x-intercept of its standard calibration curve [18,20]. Although the standard addition approach is time- and effort-demanding and requires a considerable amount of study samples, it exhibits a minimal matrix effect since it identically utilizes the same matrix of each sample for creating its own calibration curve [19,20].
The method was validated using the guidelines outlined in ICH M10 draft for endogenous compounds quantification [18], as well as the applications by Hasegawa, et al [19] and Coglianese, et al [20].

As mentioned before, the absence of a blank matrix made determining the LLOQ challenging. Therefore, we distinguish here between instrumental LLOQ (in neat blank matrix) and method LLOQ (in a real serum matrix). For instrumental LLOQ, different analyte samples were prepared water (neat blank matrix) in a concentration ranging from 0.1 to 10 ng/mL and the fluorescence difference (ΔF) were determined. The instrumental LLOQ was defined as the lowest analyte concentration that yields a fluorescence difference (ΔF) greater than 5, which was 0.5 ng/mL in the current study. Due to the absence of a blank serum sample, this instrumental LLOQ value cannot be regarded as an LLOQ value of the analyte in a real serum matrix. Therefore, we recognized method LLOQ as the lowest analyte concentration spiked to the serum sample that can be detected with acceptable accuracy (%R ± 15 %) and precision (RSD ≤ 15 %) results. The serum sample used for the method LLOQ evaluation was derived from a pool of different serum samples with low concentrations of 25(OH)D3. The pool serum sample was broken down into five equal-volume (1 mL) samples. Four spiked samples were prepared with final 25(OH)D3 added concentrations: 1, 2, 3 and 4 ng/mL, while the fourth sample was left non-spiked. The procedure for standard addition approach was completed and the accuracy and precision of three determinations (n = 3) were calculated for each spiked sample level as mentioned in experimental section. The accuracy and precision values presented in Table 1 indicated that 2 ng/mL was the lowest concentration added to the real serum samples with acceptable accuracy and precision findings.

Accuracy (%R) and precision (RSD) were evaluated by quantifying the 25(OH)D3 concentrations spiked to each spiked sample of the calibration curve. Each spiked sample was analyzed 3 times during one day for intra-day accuracy and precision calculations, and 3 times on 3 days for inter-day accuracy and precision calculations. Accepted accuracy result should be within ± 15 % of the nominal 25(OH)D3 concentration, whereas accepted precision (RSD) should be less than 15 %. The accuracy and precision values calculated for each spike sample are shown in Table 2 and are all within acceptable ranges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard addition approach calibration curves and application for estimating the endogenous 25(OH)D3 levels is autistic children and healthy children
ce:section.ce:para: As described in the experimental section, for every study sample, a standard addition calibration curve was created from which the endogenous 25(OH)D3 concentration in the sample was estimated (shown Fig. 1).

Analysis of autistic children’s samples (n = 18) and healthy children’s samples (n = 10) showed that 25(OH)D3 concentrations among autistic children varied from 6.37 to 65.11 ng/mL with a mean value of 23.80 ± 17.19, whereas 25(OH)D3 concentrations among healthy children varied from 23.70 to 78.47 ng/mL with a mean value of 50.13 ± 18.74, as shown in Fig. 5 .

25(OH)D3 concentrations were significantly lower among autistic children when compared with healthy children (P < 0.001), as shown in Table 3 . The 25(OH)D3 concentration was deficient in 8 autistic children (44.44 %), but not in any of the healthy children. In all, 6 autistic children (33.33 %) had insufficient 25(OH)D3 concentrations, compared to only 3 healthy children (30.00 %). Optimal 25(OH)D3 concentrations were found in 4 autistic children (22.22 %) and 7 healthy children (70.00 %).

In this work, a validated spectrofluorimetric methodology based on the standard addition approach for quantifying 25(OH)D3 levels in real blood samples was developed. The spectrofluorimetric methodology utilizes functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a fluorescent probe for selective quantification of 25(OH)D3 levels, which is based on measuring the quenching properties of 25(OH)D3 on a fluorescent probe. The method was validated using the guidelines outlined in ICH M10 draft for endogenous compounds quantification and was successfully applied for quantifying the serum 25(OH)D3 levels in autistic and healthy children. The results showed that autistic children had significantly lower serum 25(OH)D3 levels when compared to healthy children, suggesting an association between vitamin D insufficiency and autism.

Atiah H. Almalki: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Saleh I. Alaqel: Resources, Funding acquisition. Adnan Alharbi: Data curation, Formal analysis. Maram H. Abduljabbar: Validation. Ahmed K. Bamaga: Data curation, Investigation. Faisal Alsenani: Writing – review & editing. Sherif Ramzy: Methodology, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Northern Border University, Arar, KSA for funding this research work through the project number “NBU-FFR-2023-0174”.

Front. Psychiatry

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Vitamin D in the general population of young adults with autism in the Faroe Islands
